The training I underwent in this specialized environment was a completely different experience. It felt less like military life and more like a professional setting focused on healthcare. We wore crisp white uniforms that symbolized our commitment to service, dined in pleasant cafeterias that offered nutritious meals, and were treated with a refreshing level of respect by instructors and staff. The atmosphere was filled with the sounds of medical equipment and the discussions of future practitioners. I was surrounded by others preparing for various specialties—laboratory technicians analyzing samples, radiology technicians mastering imaging techniques, and surgical assistants learning the intricacies of the operating room. The emphasis on teamwork and patient care was palpable, making it clear that we were all training for vital roles in the medical field.

Each base camp had one of our teams, but we were the ones at Di An, focused on infrastructure support and road maintenance in a challenging environment.

I remember packing everything we owned into that old Ford, heading west like so many others during the Dust Bowl. We dreamt of orange groves and a new start, leaving Oklahoma behind us, hoping for a golden promise at the end of the road. It wasn't always easy, but we built a life under that Pacific sun, a world away from the red dirt we knew.

The desert sun beat down on my helmet, each grain of sand reflecting the heat into my eyes. I remember the weight of my pack, the canteen always half-empty, and the faces of the boys beside me, so young and scared. We were all just trying to make it to the next day, praying for a letter from home.

I remember one particular training exercise where we ascended a majestic peak, its snow-capped summit piercing the sky. The experience was unlike anything I had ever encountered before. The beauty of the mountain, with its perfectly symmetrical cone and vibrant cherry blossoms at its base, combined with the challenge of the rugged terrain, were both awe-inspiring and humbling. Each day brought new experiences, from the crisp mountain air to the breathtaking views of the surrounding landscape, and I was eager to embrace them all.
origin: I remember one particular training exercise where we went up the side of Fujiyama. That experience was unlike anything I had ever encountered before. The beauty of the mountain and the challenge of the terrain were both awe-inspiring and humbling. Each day brought new experiences, and I was eager to embrace them all.

We watched from a distance as the helicopter gunships arrived to provide support, shooting at the assailants while the refugees returned fire with their 12.5 millimeter guns.

text: Back then, you didn't question the orders coming down. I remember the weight of those eagles on my shoulders, the responsibility for the men under my command. It was a heavy burden, but one I carried with pride, hoping to guide them through the storm.

I remember arriving there, fresh out of basic. The tallgrass prairie felt endless, stretching out in every direction. It was where I learned to become a tanker, spending countless hours on those rumbling machines.

The desert sun was relentless that summer. We learned a lot about triage in the heat, the smell of antiseptic clinging to everything. Never thought I'd use any of it, but some things you just can't forget, no matter how hard you try.

The snow was piled high that year, higher than I'd ever seen it back home. We huddled together, a few of us singing carols, trying to forget where we were. Even a war couldn't steal the hope that day brought.

Back then, after basic, they sent me home with a promise and a weekend drill. Every month we'd pack our gear and head out to the armory, same faces mostly, ready to serve if Uncle Sam called again. Never thought I'd see combat after Vietnam, but those were different times.

I remember the long days in that courtroom, the tension so thick you could cut it with a knife. They said we were just following orders, but some things stay with you, no matter how hard you try to forget. Justice is supposed to be blind, but I saw a lot of things that made me question what that word really meant.

My relationship with the other members of D Company was a bit complicated. At that point, I held a position of responsibility, overseeing a small group of soldiers. I had to ensure that tasks were completed efficiently and that everyone was following orders, which meant I couldn’t be too friendly with everyone. I needed to maintain a level of authority, balancing camaraderie with the necessity of discipline, so we weren’t exactly buddies. It felt more like a mid-management role; I reported to the platoon sergeant and the platoon leader, coordinating our efforts and addressing any issues that arose within my team.

During my basic training in a sprawling military installation in Louisiana, I found myself in a world entirely new to me. With no prior military experience, I was just a tenderfoot, learning the ropes alongside others who had been in the National Guard. The air was thick with humidity, and the sound of drill sergeants echoed through the barracks as we marched in formation on the dusty grounds. I had to distinguish my left foot from my right, and it was a whirlwind of close-order drills, long hikes through the pine forests, and challenging obstacle courses. We even practiced neutralizing booby traps in the training fields, which felt both daunting and essential. The camaraderie among the recruits and the relentless pace of training made it an unforgettable experience.

I had to learn my left foot from my right, and it was a whirlwind of close-order drills, hikes, and obstacle courses. We even trained in neutralizing booby traps, which felt both daunting and essential.

The adrenaline from that day still coursed through me, and I realized how much fear and chaos could push a person to their limits.

I took his advice.

When the atomic bomb was eventually dropped on Japan, I had mixed feelings. I understood the gravity of the situation; it brought an end to a bloody conflict that could have escalated further. It was estimated that hundreds of thousands of lives would have been lost if the war continued, both among our military personnel and Japanese civilians. I felt a sense of relief that my brother, who was in the Army and on his way to the Pacific, was spared from what could have been a devastating deployment. The ship he was on turned around after the bombs were dropped, and he returned home instead of facing the horrors of war. The city that was struck was known for its beautiful coastal views and vibrant port, but in an instant, it became a scene of unimaginable destruction, with its historic buildings reduced to rubble and countless lives forever altered.

Back then, even stateside, you were always on edge. Nights were the worst; pacing those empty halls, the silence pressing down on you. Every shadow seemed to hold a threat, real or imagined.

I was born in New York, just after my parents arrived in the country. By the time I graduated high school in 1943, the war had already been raging for two years. I remember the peculiar way we were transported. Instead of the usual route through New Jersey, we went directly from Fort Meade to a ferry dock. We boarded the Queen Elizabeth, a massive troop transport ship that had never been used for civilian service. With around 16,000 men on board, we were packed into bunks six deep. I was nervous about the journey, especially since we were told to wear life vests and stay within designated areas. As we sailed away from the bustling skyline, with its towering skyscrapers and bright lights reflecting off the water, I caught a glimpse of a famous statue standing proudly in the harbor, a bittersweet reminder of home.

Our resourceful adversaries, skilled in guerrilla tactics and deeply familiar with the terrain, had cleverly used the initial chaos to distract and mislead us, allowing them to strike elsewhere while we were focused on the north. Their determination and ability to blend in with the local population made it difficult for us to anticipate their next move.

I can still picture his signature smile and the way he effortlessly connected with the troops, making us feel like we were part of something special. It was a welcome break from the tension of our daily lives.

After landing on Omaha Beach, we were attached to the First Army, but as enlisted men, we didn’t have the full picture of where we were headed next. It was a place where the echoes of war were palpable, and I can still recall the day when the Air Force bombed it for hours, wave after wave of bombers coming in to clear the way for the infantry and ground troops. The destruction was immense, leaving behind a landscape of rubble and smoke, a stark reminder of the fierce fighting that had taken place.

I graduated from high school on May 27th, 1967, a day that marked the end of one chapter and the beginning of another. As I stepped into the world, I felt the weight of the times pressing down on me. With the draft looming over everyone, I knew it was only a matter of time before I would be called to serve my country, to wear a uniform, and to face the challenges that awaited. So, I made the decision to take matters into my own hands. On July 17th, I signed up, just shy of my 18th birthday. I was still 17, and my mother had to sign the papers for me, knowing I would soon be training in distant places, learning to navigate the complexities of duty and sacrifice.

text: We were out on an outpost when the stern figures in their crisp uniforms arrived, and I immediately felt a sense of unease. Their authoritative presence and the way they moved with purpose signaled that they were searching for a young soldier, and I knew that such visits to the front lines meant something serious was afoot. When they called out his name, I held my breath, wondering if mine would be next. Thankfully, it wasn’t. They took him away, explaining that his mother had sent a letter stating he was underage. He protested, claiming she was lying, but they had proof—his birth certificate. With a firm grip, they led him off, leaving me behind, and I felt a mix of relief and concern.

text: After the news, we made our way to the large, bustling dining area filled with the clatter of trays and the aroma of freshly cooked food, where they were serving steak. I was so excited that I couldn’t even eat. After living on sea rations for so long, the thought of a hot meal was overwhelming. Long tables were lined with soldiers chatting and laughing, their plates piled high with steaming portions. But then someone came by and said, "Oh man, they cut the list in half." My heart sank; I thought my luck had run out. But then another soldier told me, "No, you’re number three now."

text: Throughout my time in England, I forged lasting friendships with the guys in my outfit. We’ve been having reunions since 1974, with the most recent one right here in Chattanooga. I even had the honor of purchasing caps for each of our veterans, proudly displaying the 752nd Engineers emblem. Our first gathering in Dyersburg, Tennessee, attracted about 150 people, including wives. Over the years, we’ve met in various locations, including a vibrant city known for its rich musical heritage, where the sounds of country music fill the air and honky-tonks line the streets, as well as places like Kingsport, Memphis, Gatlinburg, and even Columbus, Ohio, where our company commander hailed from. We share a strong camaraderie, and it’s always a joy to reconnect with old friends. Just the other day, I bumped into one of our men at a meeting, and it’s moments like that that remind me of the bonds we formed during the war.

text: Growing up, I was surrounded by family members who had served. My father and all his brothers were veterans of World War II, and my mother’s brother had also served. My father was out of the military by the time I enlisted, but his oldest brother had a distinguished career, retiring as a full colonel after 30 years of service. Their stories of bravery and sacrifice inspired me, even though I didn’t grow up on a military base. I was raised in a vibrant city known for its towering skyline, deep-dish pizza, and rich cultural scene, a place that shaped my early years, but the legacy of service was always present in my family.

The local residents, with their warm smiles and hearty laughter, invited us into their homes for Christmas dinner, which was a humbling experience. We were taken aback by their kindness, especially given the circumstances. The spirit of community and resilience was palpable, even amidst the winter chill.

The crossing took about four or five days, and I remember feeling seasick for a day or so. When we finally arrived in the Firth of Clyde in Scotland, the scenery was breathtaking. The Scottish highlands rose majestically around us, and I felt a mix of excitement and apprehension. We unloaded in Greenock and boarded troop trains that took us through England, where we were served meat pies and coffee before arriving in Southampton. From there, we boarded a converted freighter manned by a group of soldiers with distinct turbans and beards, their presence both intimidating and kind. They moved with a sense of discipline and camaraderie, and I could see the pride in their eyes as they tended to us. I slept in a hammock for the first time in my life.

The local residents, embodying the spirit of generosity and goodwill, invited us into their homes for a festive meal, which was a humbling experience. We were taken aback by their kindness, especially considering the circumstances. When it finally came time for me to return home, I had a whole different experience waiting for me in Nha Trang. After my 30-day leave, I went back to Camp Evans before heading down to a fascinating medical facility. It was located right in the heart of the city, built on what had once been a French headquarters. The French had constructed some impressive buildings, and when the military took over, they leveled parts of the city to create a more functional space for operations. The facility was bustling with activity, filled with doctors and nurses in crisp uniforms, tending to wounded soldiers and providing critical care. The sounds of medical equipment and the urgency of the staff created an atmosphere charged with both hope and tension. Tents were set up alongside the sturdy structures, accommodating the influx of patients, while the smell of antiseptic lingered in the air, a constant reminder of the life-and-death situations unfolding within its walls.

text: I was deeply tanned from my time in the humid jungles and sun-scorched fields of a distant land, and my demeanor had changed. The sounds of gunfire and the smell of smoke lingered in my mind. I was grateful to be alive, but it was hard to connect with anyone who hadn’t shared those harrowing experiences. The only people I felt comfortable around were fellow veterans. The first thing many of us did upon returning was to grow our hair long and grow beards, a way to reclaim some semblance of normalcy after the chaos and camaraderie of combat.

text: When I received my orders to return home, I felt a mix of relief and apprehension. I had been back for a brief 30-day leave six months earlier, but coming back after a year spent in a war-torn landscape was a completely different experience. The world I left behind had changed dramatically. The peace movement was in full swing, and the atmosphere was filled with the counterculture of the time—hippies, protests, and a general sense of rebellion. The memories of dense jungles, sweltering heat, and the sounds of distant gunfire were still fresh in my mind. It was a stark contrast to the carefree life I remembered before I left, where the only battles were those of everyday life, not survival.

text: When I returned to the United States, I caught sight of the Statue of Liberty once more as we docked in Staten Island. My journey was far from over, though. I was fortunate to benefit from the GI Bill, which allowed me to return to Harvard in June 1946. The GI Bill provided $500 a semester and $60 a month for living expenses, which was more than enough since Harvard's tuition was only $400. I graduated in February 1949 after accelerating my studies and then pursued a master's degree in business administration at a prestigious institution renowned for its rigorous finance curriculum, distinguished faculty, and strong emphasis on entrepreneurship and leadership, where many successful business leaders had honed their skills.

The moment I stepped off the plane, I was hit with a culture shock. The air was heavy, thick with humidity and an unfamiliar smell of spices and earth that was overwhelming. The sounds of bustling markets and distant chatter filled the air, adding to the vibrant atmosphere. Yet, I didn’t feel dread like many of my fellow soldiers. Instead, I approached it as an adventure, albeit a naive one. I knew there was a lot going on, but I didn’t fully grasp the severity of the situation I was stepping into.

The air hung thick and heavy, a blanket of humidity I could taste with every breath. Rice paddies stretched out like an endless green ocean, reflecting the harsh sun in blinding flashes. Even now, I can still feel the grit of the red dust between my teeth and hear the distant rumble.

The humidity was something fierce, even worse than Georgia in August. Rice paddies stretched out as far as the eye could see, shimmering under a sun that felt way too close. I remember thinking, "This ain't no place for a farm boy from Kansas."

To put it in perspective, this was all happening in 1967. I had arrived in the tropical region with its dense jungles and humid heat in January or early February, so I spent several months at a base surrounded by rice paddies and the sounds of distant gunfire before moving on to another location. Those early months were filled with hard work and the camaraderie of my fellow soldiers, navigating the challenges of the landscape and the complexities of the local culture, shaping my experience in a way I would carry with me long after the conflict.

The Louisiana air hung thick and heavy, even before we started drills. Between the mosquitos and the endless training, I swore I'd never want to see another cypress tree again. But looking back, it was there, surrounded by all that swampland, that I truly learned what it meant to be a soldier.

There was no outside influence guiding me toward this path; it was entirely my own decision. I didn’t have any family members who had served in the armed forces, nor did I have siblings who chose a similar route. It was a solitary choice, but one that I felt was right for me. I was drawn to the values of discipline, honor, and courage, and the idea of pushing my limits in rigorous training and challenging environments appealed to me. The commitment to serve and protect, along with the camaraderie forged through shared hardships, resonated deeply within me. It was a path that promised to shape me into a resilient individual, ready to face any challenge head-on.

text: The Madam always had a smile, even when the MPs came sniffing around. I remember the piano player tinkling away, trying to drown out the sounds upstairs. Some of the boys said they found solace there, a temporary escape from the war.

After my time in Quang Tri, I learned about the history of our unit from the short-timers who had been there before me. They shared stories of the fierce battles that had taken place, particularly in areas characterized by rows of tall, slender trees with broad leaves, their trunks marked by deep cuts to extract a milky sap. The landscape was often dotted with the remnants of old processing facilities, where the harvested latex was transformed into sheets. Our company had even earned a unit citation for our efforts, a badge of honor that we were proud to wear. By the time I arrived, those who had fought in those battles were nearing the end of their service, and I was left to carry on the legacy they had built.

After basic training, I wasn’t immediately deployed. Instead, I spent another eight weeks at a sprawling military base known for its focus on engineering and construction skills, nestled in the heart of the Midwest. The training environment was a blend of classroom instruction and extensive fieldwork, surrounded by vast training areas and facilities designed for hands-on learning. I learned how to build bridges and, just as importantly, how to destroy them. The experience was immersive, with a strong emphasis on teamwork and practical application, preparing me for the challenges ahead. I found a sense of purpose in the work I was doing. Each day brought new lessons and experiences, shaping me into the soldier I was becoming.

It was hands-on training that prepared me for the challenges ahead, and I found a sense of purpose in the work I was doing. Each day brought new lessons and experiences, shaping me into the soldier I was becoming.

We were preparing to face a land known for its fierce battles and resilient spirit, a place where the sounds of gunfire and the cries of the wounded echoed in the distance. The thought of navigating through the challenges of a foreign terrain, filled with both beauty and danger, loomed large in my mind.

It was a wake-up call for some in my class who didn’t take it seriously; they lost their commissions, which reinforced the importance of hard work and dedication. The atmosphere was filled with the sounds of drills and the sight of soldiers in uniform, all dedicated to mastering the art of military transport.

text: Despite the initial tension, a few of us outsiders bonded over our shared experience. We were all in the same boat, preparing the unit for deployment to a tropical region known for its dense jungles and challenging terrain. We went through rigorous training together, learning to handle the new M-16 rifles that had just been introduced, a shift from the heavier M-14s we had previously used. The air was thick with humidity, and the sounds of distant gunfire echoed in our minds as we practiced our drills. We were all up to date on our vaccinations, knowing we would face not only the enemy but also the threats of disease in the sweltering heat. We spent a couple of months in Georgia getting ready for our mission, honing our skills in the sweltering sun, before we were granted another 30-day leave to return home.
